notes.anchor: If you and your children are well, it is well; I and my army are in good health
notes.note: The formal health-formula expanded for a public dispatch addressed to the whole state; "I and my army" signals that the message comes from a commander in the field.

notes.anchor: I call gods and men to witness, senators
notes.note: "Senators" renders patres conscripti, the formal mode of addressing the Senate; this letter is Lepidus's open dispatch to the magistrates, Senate, and people of Rome justifying his conduct.

notes.anchor: had not fortune wrung from my hands a policy of my own
notes.note: Lepidus's euphemism for his junction with Antony on 29 May 43 BC, which he presents as forced upon him; the Senate was unconvinced and on 30 June declared him a public enemy.
Lepidus blames a mutiny (seditio) of his soldiers — who, he claims, insisted on sparing their fellow-citizens in Antony's camp — for compelling him to merge his army with Antony's rather than fight.
